content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Moon (2009) is a masterclass in minimalist sci-fi and a truly haunting character study.

**Score:** 119
**Body:** This is one of those films that proves you don't need a massive budget to tell a profound and gripping story. Sam Rockwell's performance is absolutely incredible—he carries the entire film on his shoulders.  
It's a quiet, intelligent, and deeply isolating movie that explores identity and sanity in a way that sticks with you long after it's over.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Directed by Duncan Jones, David Bowies son. I think it’s a fantastic movie!
  - Forgot about that! It's been ages since I've seen it. Gotta watch again.
2. Fantastic score too. One of Clint Mansell's best.
  - High Rise is another Clint gem as well. I listen to both while I'm working.
3. No need to use AI, I'd appreciate you telling us what you think in your own words
  - Why would someone use AI for three sentences?
4. I feel like this movie has become a little bit forgotten since Interstellar came out, but it was quite popular before then. I'll have to rewatch it one of those days, I remember it being quite funny without being a comedy as well.
5. Sam Rockwell's performance in Moon is so fascinating as clones with different personalities.
6. [removed]
7. Everything about the movie works. The concept is fantastic, blending a bunch of different types of plausible Sci-Fi. The script is tight and nearly flawless. Rockwell is pitch-perfect in both roles in every scene, and Spacey's delivery balances the flatness of an AI with just the right notes of emotion. The tension is perfectly balanced. The mystery is unraveled really well. The music is brilliant. The production design is amazing, especially for the budget. I was so glad to have seen it in theaters when it came out.

## 2. Please suggest Christmas themed movies to watch this Holiday Season? Aside from Home Alone

**Score:** 38
**Body:** Hey guys so I've watched Home Alone and all the sequels many times already. looking forward to watching more christmas themed movies this year

some of the movies i've watched already:

Red One, A Christmas Carol, Polar Express, Problem Child, Goonies, Spirited Away.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Muppet Christmas Carol--best adaptation of the Dickens classic, Emmett Otter's Jugband Christmas, if you are looking for cuteness.
2. Scrooged (1988)
3. Elf (2003).  It’s a Wonderful Life (1946).  Miracle on 34th Street (1947).  National Lampoon's Christmas Vacation (1989).
4. While you were sleeping!
5. Trading places
6. I'd recommend Klaus (2019), an animated movie. I'm not a Christian so I haven't usually have seen many Christmas themed movies but Klaus was definitely special and for me one of the best stories ever told.
7. A Christmas Story
8. Elf 

The Grinch

Family Stone

The Holiday

Die Hard
9. The Holdovers (2023)
10. The Santa Clause. I'm not a big Tim Allen fan, but this movie is great.

## 3. Movies where every frame looks like a Wallpaper

**Score:** 34
**Body:** Suggest me films so visually stunning that every frame could be a wallpaper. Some examples are The Batman, Blade Runner 2049, Tron Legacy.

FYI I have already seen Mad Max Fury Road, Dune 1 & 2, Watchmen, The Grand Budapest Hotel, Interstellar, 1917, Arrival.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Fountain although I still have no idea what that movie was about.
2. Barry Lyndon (1975)
3. The Cell
4. Hear me out...Speed Racer (2008)
5. The Fall
6. Dune series.....or I'll say all Denis Villeneuve movies
7. Loving Vincent
8. Lawrence of Arabia
9. Melancholia, Marie Antoinette (yes, I love Kirsten Dunst)
10. Here you go:

https://www.everysingleframe.com

## 4. Heist Movies

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Heist movies are my absolute all time favorite genre, but i feel like i’ve seen them all. Anyone got some i might not have seem yet?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Nothing groundbreaking but Logan Lucky was fun
  - Logan Lucky was such a pleasant surprise. 

Adding Baby Driver to the list.
2. The Killing (1956)
3. Sneakers (1992) I watched it recently and it totally holds up. 
4. Spike Lee's Inside Man.
5. I guess you've already seen Heat and Point Break.
6. Den of Thieves is decent
7. The Sting
8. How to Blow Up a Pipeline (2022)
9. Thief and Sexy Beast come to mind

## 5. Any movies like Adventureland with that small town vibe?

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I just rewatched Adventureland and forgot how much I love that kind of movie. The small town summer feeling, the awkward relationships, the mellow pacing, it just hits perfectly. I’ve already seen the other stuff by the same director, so I’m looking for something new that gives off a similar vibe.

Doesn’t have to be another coming of age story, just something that provides a mix of nostalgia and small town atmosphere.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Way Way Back
  - I haven’t watched The Way Way Back yet, which is surprising since I’m a huge Sam Rockwell fan. Will watch it for sure, thanks!
2. Snack shack
3. The Perks of Being a Wallflower (2012)
4. The Kings of Summer (2013) will scratch that itch
5. Grandview USA
6. I love Adventureland, it’s such a hidden gem. Brilliant soundtrack too.
7. Napoleon Dynamite

Stand By Me

The Sand Lot

Dazed and Confused

Groundhog Day

Juno

Pleasantville

Hot Rod 

Tried to do a range of the genres and vibes but they're all set in small towns
8. SLC Punk explores the punk scene of Salt Lake City with characters that just come, make their mark on the narrator, and go. Bittersweet but it's fun to just vibe with these characters for a short time


I hear good things about Licorice Pizza

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. The Cabin in the Woods.

**Score:** 599

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This definitely one of the best movies to watch knowing nothing about it.
  - This is exactly what happened to me. Didn't have a clue about the movie and was blown away. Absolutely love this film.
    - Love this film soup to nuts. I remember reading it was complete and shelved for a few years before being released.
  - I was literally browsing one day thinking to myself "man I'm in the mood for a shitty cliche horror movie with a cabin in the woods" and when I saw the title, I laughed my ass off and immediately went in cold

Suffice to say I was pleasantly suprised
  - Its a kind of special movie that you cant really advertise. Not knowing anything and seeing it was one of my most favorite movie experiences.
2. Really liked the characters played by Richard Jenkins and Bradley Whitford.
  - Bradley Whitford needs to be in more. Loved West Wing and he’s in two of my favorite horror movies, this and Get Out. I’d like to see him do more horror.
3. Always felt like the Hellraiser guy with the spherical machine was probably ok. Like, up to a point, you could probably at least have had a decent conversation with him
4. Great movie to do a double feature with Tucker and Dale vs Evil
5. Wait. That's not fair. I had zombies, too.  
  
Yes, you did. Yes, you had zombies. But this is "Zombie Redneck Torture Family," see? They're entirely separate species. Like the difference between an elephant and an elephant seal.

## 2. What's the best lawyer movie?

**Score:** 543
**Body:** I'll start. Love My Cousin Vinny. It's what you would want to do in Court but can't and surprisingly pretty accurate in terms of actual trial practice. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. To Kill a Mockingbird? Does that make me old?
  - Came looking for this.

That does not mean you're old. It means you have great taste.
2. A Few Good Men
3. I think this is an interesting trivia of My Cousin Vinny (from IMDB):

”Director Jonathan Lynn actually has a law degree and insisted the film's legal proceedings be realistic. In fact, many attorneys and law professors have praised the film for its accurate depiction of trial strategy and courtroom procedure, especially with regards to presenting expert witnesses at trial. Additionally, the film has been screened at some law schools to illustrate courtroom procedures.”
4. The Verdict
5. OP, your pick is dead on balls accurate.
6. A Time To Kill
7. A Civil Action with Travolta and Duvall.
8. Liar Liar
9. If you want accuracy for the pathos of \*most\* lawyers, Michael Clayton.  Boy, did Clooney nail that, especially the very very ending in the cab.

## 3. "The Campaign" is such an amazing satirical movie, and probably my favorite Will Ferrell  character. He was so, so good in this movie.

**Score:** 309
**Body:** This movie is nothing but [one classic scene](https://youtu.be/QCeuQSh7dXU), [after another](https://youtu.be/6Ac3XAjq82E), [after another](https://youtu.be/N-poTVWtIkA), [after another](https://youtu.be/YxWVEt7ytfE) (his last line in this scene where he looks at Marty Huggins after he just embarrassed the shit out of him at that civility brunch with that video he made is great. He just looks at him sternly and says "Welcome to the fucking show." I love that part so much. [And this scene ](https://youtu.be/gMCiqs4sKfA)that was clearly poking fun at Dick Cheney's, um, hunting accident is hysterical. The writers of this movie were absolutely top-notch, in every way. Perfect satire movie, in my opinion. I really think this is my favorite Will Ferrell performance in a comedy movie, and that is saying a lot with how many hilarious characters he has played. I don't know why, but this movie has me in stitches every single time I throw it on.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Is this the movie where that baby gets punched in the face in slow motion
  - Yes..  and it's never failed to crack me up.
2. *If you're still mad that I wore Crocs to moms funeral ...like I've told you a million times I'm sorry. She would have wanted it that way, she was casual*

Zach Galifanakas is too funny.
3. “I, Fucked, Up”
4. That’s what nuts feel like. Touch it.
5. “Filipino tilt-a-whirl operators are this nations backbone”
6. “And I come here and find you playing heehaw with the fuck around gang!”
7. Schools is this nation’s backbone
  - Filipino tilt-a-whirl operators ARE this nation's backbone!
8. Ass and titties, ass and titties

## 4. Is Lawrence of Arabia one of the best films ever made? Is it worth watching?

**Score:** 248

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes and yes.
  - And it's best enjoyed on the biggest screen you can access.  This is not a film to be watched on a phone or tablet.
    - David Lynch would concur, definitely not your telephone
  - /thread
2. “The trick, William Potter, is not minding that it hurts”
3. Masterpiece.  Some of the most breath taking cinematography in film history
4. "LAWRENCE? LAWRENCE WHAT? OF ARABIA?!? That name sounds like royalty. Are you ROYALTY?!?!" - SGT. Hartman
5. 7 academy awards, 3 hours and 47 minutes long, over 1000 cast and extras, a soundtrack the defined the genre, themes of identity and duality, heroism, the irony of imperialism, sexuality, pride, violence, covering the vast sweeping landscapes of the Arabian campaign, and not One. Single. Speaking. Female role. Now that's cinema.
6. It’s everything an epic film should be and more.
7. Yes and Yes. Got to see it on the big screen earlier this year and it was breathtaking.

## 5. What's your favorite Saddest scene in films?

**Score:** 53

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The beginning montage from Up
2. Rocket Raccoon's orgin story in Guardians 
  - "You know what this gut punch needs? Cute baby animals being mangled."
3. BROOKS WAS HERE.

.      ..   .                               and so was red
4. Planes, trains, and automobiles. John Candy's, I like me speech.
5. The butterfly scene in JoJo Rabbit.
6. Fight against the Sadness, Artax. Please, you’re letting the Sadness of the Swamps get to you. You have to try. You have to care. For me. You’re my friend. I love you.
7. Children of Men … When the battle resumed after a moment of stillness when the soldiers recognized the new born child.
8. Bing bong fading away
9. "Where are his glasses? He can't see without his glasses!"

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What were the popular visual/stylistic horror cinema techniques prior to the "Elevated Age" (2013-)?

**Score:** 11
**Body:** **\[Disclaimer:** I'm only using "elevated horror" here as a shorthand to discuss the horror films made after roughly 2013, the A24/Neon years. I'm not interested in debating the term's validity as a descriptor of quality, I only mean to designate the time period I'm discussing, which hopefully is evident.**\]**

 In reading the Hollywood Reporter's review of Chris Stuckman's SHELBY OAKS -- which chides the filmmaker for essentially stitching together remakes of his favorite horror scenes -- I came across this line:

>There are some neat little stylistic flourishes that one can appreciate — a gliding camera here, **a sudden switch from day to night there** — until one realizes that, wait a second, those are things that happened in other recent horror movies.

And it reminded me of the trailer for Oz Perkins' upcoming KEEPER, which also features a match-cut from day/night -- a technique I've seen in quite a few recent horror movies, which again was popularized, as best I can tell, in Ari Aster's HEREDTIARY. Which was a film that introduced a spate of visual/stylistic tools that  have been picked up by other other horror directors in the intervening years. 

Most ubiquitously, the ["upside down shot"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8ZSTvOWajFk) which Aster used both in Hereditary and Midsommar -- and which was lovingly referenced in a dozen movies ranging from Lee Cronin's HOLE IN THE GROUND, Nia DaCosta's [CANDYMAN](https://www.filmograph.tv/project/candyman) and most recently Parker Finn's [SMILE 1](https://www.reddit.com/r/SmileMovie/comments/1gplled/upside_down_skyline/) *and* [2](https://youtu.be/Fy7kaI3GKzo?si=w1Q4IPaPwGLvOiAq&t=67) \-- has become a staple of horror cinema to show a world that's "wrong". Upside down New York or Chicago. upside down idyllic Swedish Woods or Irish countryside. It's a succinct visual way to communicate that the world we're entering isn't what it seems.

And I feel like we could probably list a bunch of shots, edits and camera moves that horror in the Elevated Horror Age (again, for lack of a better word) that filmmakers use and overuse.

**But what were those shots, edits and camera moves in previous ages?** In the 2000s horror cinema what were the techniques that were enthusiastically copied? The 90s were notoriously paltry in terms of horror, but was there any popular visual techniques that were referenced a lot? 

Or is this relentless visual intertextuality a more pronounced dynamic in modern horror due to the wide availability and volume of horror movies in the last 15 years?

The one technique that comes to mind from the 1980s is Sam Raimi's 'racing POV shot' from EVIL DEAD.

Thoughts?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think there is a style I really loved from the 70s and 80's, could be seen as its own version of elevated horror. 

It's the documentary style. Not documentary a sense of  it having a lower standard of photography and framing, I'm talking about the slice of life naturalism of the writing. To the point of boredom. And the ordinariness of the staging. 

It occurred to me that it was a style after playing the FMV game 'Imortality'. I recognised instantly the style they were going for. 

The first movie that springs to mind is 'The Exorcist'. Director William Freadkin wanted a movie that would terrify Atheists that don't believe in demonic possession. I think he acheived this through the slow ordinariness of the people and the slow moments that set up the film before the horror is introduced. 

The other film is Invasion of the Body Snatchers. I was pleasantly surprised; expecting some schlocky romp. You see a married couple, these intimate moments of daily routine, dinner parties, going work. It stars Donald Sotherland. The alien possession theme is in one sense ridiculous, in another sense made more terrifying because you believe the people in the movie are real people. It's never to be taken on face value either. There is always an allegory behind the horror. 

Don't Look Now. Again; Donald Sutherland. Based on a Daphne Du Morier novel (the novelist behind several Hitchcock adaptations). Clear influence of Anti Christ by Lars Von Trier. 

And special shout out to Possession (1981). Sam Neil was crazy in that. Also captures the weird, paranoid atmosphere of West Berlin when the wall was still standing. Although it's really a movie about divorce.
  - Alien does this extremely well, due to the 70s cinema verite-type audio mixing with people talking over each other, etc. which isn’t exactly camera shots but still counts stylistically I think.
  - Shout out to Halloween’s pervy POV shots that I think fit in this vein.
    - A lot of people point to Black Christmas as the progenitor of the POV style that was also used in Halloween, but the opening to the 1971 film Blood and Lace is remarkably similar to Halloween. The killer approaches the house at night, gains entry through the kitchen, pulls a weapon out of the drawer, slowly ascends the staircase, and enters the bedroom to kill a naked female. The biggest stylistic difference is that Blood and Lace isn't done in one unbroken shot; it occasionally cuts to an objective POV, although you still don't see the killer. But once you see it, it's difficult to imagine it wasn't an influence.
  - Possession's most noticeable technique to accomplish its atmosphere is the abundant orbit shots.
2. I personally most love the bold colored lighting and sets of 70s Italian horror - not using “giallo”, here, since genre purists know Suspiria (1977) technically doesn’t count as such, but a LOT of it is giallo. A lot of these films would signal that things were going haywire by washing scenes out in otherworldly tones - red being the most natural go-to for a murder scene but also yellows, greens or purples being used to great effect. Deep Red and Blood and Black Lace are two other great examples of this, and moving out of Italy, Hausu also had some great wild color choices.

It’s something you still see replicated - Mandy is an extreme example, I think the brief exposition scene between Nic Cage and Bill Duke’s characters is literally the only scene with “normal” lighting. It’s also in a lot of Malignant, which I think people still write off as “the movie with the wild ending” but what really works, for me, as a gonzo supernatural slasher with a great sense of mayhem and fun. In a more serious example, Red Rooms has deep red lighting in a very stripped-down horrific scene where >!two characters watch an offscreen video of a horrific torture and murder!<. It adds to the already thick horror built just through the sounds and the actress’ reactions.

It’s something I’d definitely love to see used more in modern films, as it just adds so much visual flair. But it can certainly be overused, too, in a way that just makes a film look kinda dumb - it’s not actual horror, but Rob Zombie’s *The Munsters* is a good example of the wacky lighting just going into garish and annoying territory.
3. Bit of a side note, but I’d be more than happy if I never saw the upside down shot again. It’s just so low effort and the meaning is so obvious. I want to be feeling “oh no, the rules are going out the window and now nothing is what it seems”, but instead I’m sighing wearily and thinking “here we go again…”.

If there are any modern examples of the shot being done well, I’d be genuinely interested. But that Midsommar one is not it. Horror movies routinely begin with a journey to a place where the rules of the civilized world aren't in effect. Putting an upside-down shot on top of that is really spoon-feeding the audience.
  - Listen, there's obviously no accounting for taste, so if you intuitively don't like the Midsommar shot, there's probably nothing I can say to you to convince you otherwise. That being said, I think it's a wonderful and - at the time - innovative bit of stylism. It feels obvious *now* because it's been aggressively overdone.

But to me it's sort of like chiding Requiem For A Dream for its Snorri-cam sequence saying it's a lazy shortcut to show a character's mental disorientation.

But of course it's only lazy now because it's become a part of filmmaking grammar. I think it's helpful to put things in their historical context. 

The first time a novel used the phrase "She let out a breath she didn't realize she was holding" it wasn't a cliché. It was an artful and sympathetic descriptor of a character's tense mental state.
    - Good points!
  - It’s not from a horror movie but the shot from the first Black Panther where killmonger has beaten TChalla and taken the throne room is a genuinely awesome  moment.

https://youtu.be/Gtepp-acYOY?si=kuIrrT8XnSfk5Dfl

---
